9	/-!
10	# The `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` substrate (A.1.c.SubT)
11	
12	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
14	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
15	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
16	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).
17	
18	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
19	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
20	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
21	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
22	
23	1. a binary tensor-product operation
24	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
25	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
26	3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
27	   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.
28	
29	At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
30	is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
31	as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
32	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
33	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
34	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
35	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.
36	
37	## Status (iter-202 Lane TS — file-skeleton scaffold)
38	
39	This file is the **iter-202 Lane TS** file-skeleton: each of the 4 pinned
40	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
41	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`) with a
42	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-203+ work: the `tensorObj` definition lifts
43	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through sheafification, and the consumer
44	`PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` then closes the residual `addCommGroup`
45	sorry of `RelPicFunctor.lean` L235.
46	
47	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
48	
49	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (def) — the substrate binary
50	   operation `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`,
51	   lifting `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` on underlying presheaves
52	   composed with sheafification on the small Zariski site.
53	   Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`.
54	
55	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality` (def) — the
56	   functorial action of `⊗` on morphisms: a pair `f : M ⟶ M'`, `g : N ⟶ N'`
57	   determines `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`.
58	   Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`.

61	   monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X` with tensor `⊗`, unit

64	   Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`.
65	
66	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (def) — the

68	   `Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)`, built via the

71	   Per blueprint `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`.

100	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
101	
102	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
103	
104	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
105	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
106	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
107	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
108	
109	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
110	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
111	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
112	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
113	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
114	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
115	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
116	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
117	
118	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
119	
120	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
121	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
122	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
123	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
124	
125	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
126	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
127	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
128	sheafification. -/
129	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
130	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
131	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
132	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
133	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
134	
135	/-! ## §2. The monoidal-category structure -/
136	
137	/-- **Monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
138	
139	The bifunctor `⊗_X` of `tensorObj_functoriality`, with unit object the
140	structure sheaf `O_X`, associator `α`, left/right unitors `λ`, `ρ`, and
141	braiding `β` inherited from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal` under sheafification,
142	satisfies the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms; hence `Scheme.Modules X`
143	carries a canonical (symmetric) monoidal-category structure.
144	
145	Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
146	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body discharges the coherence axioms
147	affine-locally (each reduces to the analogous identity for the tensor product
148	of modules over a commutative ring) and assembles the
149	`CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory` instance. -/
150	noncomputable instance monoidalCategory {X : Scheme.{u}} :
151	    MonoidalCategory (X.Modules) :=
152	  sorry

154	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
155	
156	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
157	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
158	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
159	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
160	
161	/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**
162	
163	If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
164	their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
165	`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed
166	`sorry`; the iter-203+ body trivialises both factors on a common affine cover
167	and uses that the tensor product of two free rank-one modules is free of rank
168	one. -/
169	lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
170	    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
171	    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) :=
172	  sorry
173	
174	/-- **Inverse of an invertible module.**
175	
176	Every line bundle `L : X.Modules` has a two-sided tensor inverse: there is a
177	locally-trivial `Linv : X.Modules` (the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)`) together with
178	a tensor isomorphism `L ⊗_X Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)`. Per blueprint
179	`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`;
180	the iter-203+ body builds the dual and the contraction isomorphism, which is an
181	isomorphism affine-locally on a trivialising cover. -/
182	lemma exists_tensorObj_inverse {X : Scheme.{u}} {L : X.Modules}
183	    (hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L) :
184	    ∃ Linv : X.Modules, LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial Linv ∧
185	      Nonempty (tensorObj L Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)) :=
186	  sorry
187	
188	/-- **Restriction of `⊗` to the `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier.**
189	
190	For `S`-schemes `C, T`, the bifunctor `⊗_{C ×_S T}` restricts to the subtype
191	`LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` of locally-trivial modules on `C ×_S T`, with unit
192	the structure sheaf and the dual as two-sided inverse. Per blueprint
193	`lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`. -/
194	noncomputable def tensorObjOnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S)
195	    (L L' : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT :=
196	  ⟨tensorObj L.carrier L'.carrier,
197	    tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial L.isLocallyTrivial L'.isLocallyTrivial⟩

199	end Modules

202

206	`Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`.**

208	For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test object
209	`πT : T ⟶ S`, the relative Picard quotient

211	carries a canonical `AddCommGroup` structure with addition the descent of the
212	tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` of `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`, neutral

5	## Priority Rule

7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

124

127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).

143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.

148	These run automatically between phases:

151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  \leanok
255	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
257	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
258	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
259	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
260	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
261	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
262	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
263	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
264	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
265	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
266	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
267	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
268	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
269	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{theorem}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
276	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
278	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
279	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
280	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
281	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
282	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
283	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
284	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
285	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
286	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
287	\end{theorem}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
291	  % NOTE (plan iter-204): there is deliberately NO proof-block \leanok here.
292	  % The Lean instance AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory has a
293	  % `:= sorry` body (intentional contamination guard; deferred-large monoidal
294	  % coherence, to be transported through the sheafification-forget reflective
295	  % adjunction). A proof-block \leanok would assert "proof closed, no sorry",
296	  % which is false. The erroneous proof-block \leanok that the deterministic
297	  % sync_leanok failed to remove across iter-203 was stripped here by the plan
298	  % agent as a correction of an over-claiming marker. The statement-block
299	  % \leanok (the sorry-bodied decl exists) is correct and untouched.
300	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
301	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
302	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
303	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
304	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
305	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
306	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
307	  identities yield global coherence identities on
308	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
309	  same affine descent argument.
310	\end{proof}
311	
312	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
313	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
314	
315	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
316	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
317	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
318	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
319	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
320	monoidal-category structure of
321	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
322	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
323	
324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
326	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
328	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
329	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
330	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
331	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
332	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
333	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
334	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
335	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
340	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
341	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
342	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
343	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
344	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
345	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
346	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
347	\end{proof}
348	
349	\begin{lemma}
350	[Inverse of an invertible module]
351	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
353	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
354	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
355	  bundle. The dual
356	  \[
357	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
358	  \]
359	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
360	  isomorphism
361	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
362	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
363	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
364	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
365	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
366	\end{lemma}
367	
368	\begin{proof}
369	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
370	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
371	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
372	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
373	  The contraction map
374	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
375	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
376	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
377	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
378	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
379	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
380	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
381	\end{proof}

383	\begin{lemma}
384	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
385	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
386	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
387	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
388	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
389	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
390	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
391	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
392	  \[
393	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
394	    \;\;=\;\;
395	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
396	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
397	  \]
398	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
400	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
401	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
402	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
403	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
404	\end{lemma}
405	
406	\begin{proof}
407	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
408	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
409	  Direct from
410	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
411	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
412	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
413	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
414	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
415	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
416	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
417	  monoidal-category structure of
418	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
419	  the same affine descent argument.
420	\end{proof}

422	\begin{lemma}
423	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
424	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
425	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
426	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
427	  functor
428	  \[
429	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
430	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
431	  \]
432	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
433	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
434	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
435	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
436	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
437	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
438	  canonical isomorphism
439	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
440	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
441	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
442	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
443	\end{lemma}
444	
445	\begin{proof}
446	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
447	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
448	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
449	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
450	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
451	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
452	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
453	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
454	  same descent argument as in
455	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
456	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
457	  same cover.
458	\end{proof}

460	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
461	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
462	
463	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
464	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
465	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
466	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
467	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
468	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
469	
470	\begin{theorem}
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
474	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
476	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
477	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
478	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
479	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
480	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
481	
482	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
483	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
484	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
485	  % groups)
486	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
487	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
488	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
489	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
490	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
491	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
492	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
493	  % \end{dfn}
494	  %  [\dots]
495	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
496	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
497	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
498	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
499	  % topologies  by
500	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
501	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
502	  %  \end{dfn}"
503	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
504	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
505	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
506	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
507	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
508	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
509	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
510	  \[
511	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
512	  \]
513	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
514	  abelian-group structure with addition
515	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
516	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
517	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
518	  inverse
519	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
520	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
521	  supplied by the substrate of
522	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
523	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
524	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
525	  map
526	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
527	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
528	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
529	\end{theorem}
530	
531	\begin{proof}
532	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
533	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
534	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
535	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
536	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
537	  By
538	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
539	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
540	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
541	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
542	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
543	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
544	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
545	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
546	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
547	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
548	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
549	
550	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
551	  functor
552	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
553	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
554	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
555	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
556	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
557	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
558	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
559	
560	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
561	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
562	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
563	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
564	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
565	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
566	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
567	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
568	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
569	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
570	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
571	\end{proof}

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). iter-187 supplies the missing
96	predicate as a project-side definition via local trivialisation on an affine
97	open cover (see `IsLocallyTrivial` below), and refines `OnProduct` to the
98	corresponding subtype.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- A sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules `M` is **locally trivial of rank one** if every
104	point `x : X` has an affine open neighbourhood `U` on which the restriction
105	`M|_U` is isomorphic to the structure sheaf-as-module `𝒪_U`. This is the
106	project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate on
107	`Scheme.Modules`: line bundles (invertible sheaves) are exactly the
108	`IsLocallyTrivial` modules, by Stacks tag 01HK / Hartshorne II §6. Local
109	trivialisability is the structural content that survives base change along
110	`f : Y ⟶ X` (Stacks 01HH) and is the source of the invertibility-preservation
111	step for `pullbackAlongProjection`.
112	
113	The witness is bundled as `Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit _)`:
114	the existence of a sheaf-of-modules isomorphism over the affine chart. -/
115	def IsLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
116	  ∀ x : X, ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
117	    Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf)
118	
119	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
120	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
121	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
122	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
123	Picard functor at `T`.
124	
125	iter-187: refined to the subtype of `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules` cut out
126	by the project-side predicate `IsLocallyTrivial` (locally trivial of rank one).
127	The carrier is now genuinely the line-bundle subobject of `Scheme.Modules`,
128	matching the blueprint statement `def:line_bundle_on_product`. Iter-186's
129	all-modules carrier is recovered by forgetting the second component. -/
130	def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
131	  { M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M }
132	
133	/-- Projection from a line bundle on the product to its underlying
134	sheaf of modules. -/
135	abbrev OnProduct.carrier {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
136	    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules := L.1
137	
138	/-- A line bundle on the product is locally trivial of rank one. -/
139	lemma OnProduct.isLocallyTrivial {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
140	    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : IsLocallyTrivial L.carrier := L.2
141	
142	/-- **Stacks 01HH (pullback of an invertible sheaf is invertible).** For any
143	morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ X` and any locally-trivial sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules
144	`M`, the pulled-back module `f^* M : Y.Modules` is again locally trivial.
145	
146	The proof unfolds an affine open cover trivialising `M`, pulls back along `f`,
147	and observes that `f^*` commutes with restriction along open immersions (the
148	`pullback`/`restrictFunctor` natural isomorphism in
149	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) so that the trivialising chart for
150	`M|_U` becomes a trivialising chart for `(f^* M)|_(f⁻¹ U)`. The full chart-chase
151	involves the natural-isomorphism `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` plus the
152	preservation of `SheafOfModules.unit` under pullback (Mathlib
153	`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackFree`'s
154	`unitToPushforwardObjUnit`). Iter-187 wraps this chase as a named typed sorry
155	(helper-budget = 1); iter-188+ instantiates the chart-chase. -/
156	lemma IsLocallyTrivial.pullback {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X) {M : X.Modules}
157	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
158	    IsLocallyTrivial ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M) := by
159	  intro y
160	  obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU_aff, eM⟩ := hM (f.base y)
161	  have hyU' : y ∈ f ⁻¹ᵁ U := hxU
162	  obtain ⟨V, hV_aff, hyV, hVU⟩ := exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset hyU'
163	  refine ⟨V, hyV, hV_aff, ?_⟩
164	  -- Stacks 01HH chart-chase: factor `V.ι ≫ f` through `U` using
165	  -- `Scheme.Hom.resLE`, then transport through `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`
166	  -- and `pullbackComp` to identify the pullback at `V` with the pullback of
167	  -- the unit on `U` to `V`. Close via `eM` and `pullbackObjUnitToUnit`
168	  -- (which is an iso since `Opens.map g.base` is final via the
169	  -- `RepresentablyFlat` instance).
170	  obtain ⟨eM⟩ := eM
171	  set g : (V : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := f.resLE U V hVU with hg_def
172	  have hg_comp : g ≫ U.ι = V.ι ≫ f := Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι f hVU
173	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map g.base).Final :=
174	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
175	  refine ⟨?_⟩
176	  -- 1: ((pullback f).obj M).restrict V.ι ≅ (pullback V.ι).obj ((pullback f).obj M)
177	  let i1 :=
178	    (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback V.ι).app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M)
179	  -- 2: ≅ (pullback (V.ι ≫ f)).obj M
180	  let i2 := (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp V.ι f).app M
181	  -- 3: ≅ (pullback (g ≫ U.ι)).obj M  (using V.ι ≫ f = g ≫ U.ι)
182	  let i3 := (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hg_comp.symm).app M
183	  -- 4: ≅ (pullback g).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
184	  let i4 := ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp g U.ι).symm).app M
185	  -- 5: ≅ (pullback g).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
186	  let i5 := (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).mapIso
187	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M)
188	  -- 6: ≅ (pullback g).obj (unit_U)
189	  let i6 := (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).mapIso eM
190	  -- 7: ≅ unit_V  (pullback of unit is unit via the Mathlib morphism, which is
191	  -- iso since the underlying `Opens.map g.base` is Final)
192	  let i7 := asIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit g.toRingCatSheafHom)
193	  exact i1 ≪≫ i2 ≪≫ i3 ≪≫ i4 ≪≫ i5 ≪≫ i6 ≪≫ i7

195	/-! ## §2. The pullback functor along the projection
196	
197	For a morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ Z` and an invertible `O_Z`-module `M`, the
198	pullback `f^* M` is an invertible `O_Y`-module (the pullback of an
199	open-cover-trivialisation of `M` trivialises `f^* M`, and the pullback of a free
200	module of rank one is free of rank one — Stacks tag 01HH). Specialising to the
201	projection `π_T = pullback.snd πC πT : C ×_S T ⟶ T` gives the pullback map
202	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`. Mathlib's underlying `Scheme.Modules.pullback`
203	functor (in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) supplies the pullback on
204	the full category of sheaves of modules; iter-175+ will restrict it to
205	invertible sheaves once the `OnProduct` carrier is unpacked.
206	
207	Blueprint reference: `def:pullback_along_projection` (Kleiman §2; Stacks tag
208	01HH). -/
209	
210	/-- **Pullback along the projection** `π_T : C ×_S T ⟶ T` of a relative
211	curve. Sends a *line bundle on `T`* — i.e. an `O_T`-module `N` equipped with a
212	proof `hN : IsLocallyTrivial N` — to the line bundle `π_T^* N` on `C ×_S T`,
213	induced by the `Scheme.Modules.pullback` functor on `Scheme.Modules` together
214	with the preservation step `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` (Stacks 01HH).
215	
216	iter-187: refined to consume the `OnProduct`-style subtype on the source: the
217	underlying carrier is the direct application of Mathlib's
218	`Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)` functor at `N`, and the
219	locally-trivial witness comes from the project-side helper
220	`IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` (Stacks 01HH, named typed sorry pending the affine
221	chart chase). -/
222	noncomputable def pullbackAlongProjection {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
223	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (N : T.Modules) (hN : IsLocallyTrivial N) :
224	    OnProduct πC πT :=
225	  ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N,
226	    hN.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)⟩

229	
230	The composition law for line-bundle pullback (Stacks tag 01HG) says that for any
231	composable morphisms `f : X ⟶ Y, g : Y ⟶ Z` and any line bundle `M ∈ Pic(Z)`,
232	the natural isomorphism `(g ∘ f)^* M ≅ f^* g^* M` makes the pullback functor
233	into a (pseudo-)functor `Sch^op ⥤ Cat`.

236	base-change morphism `g_C := id_C ×_S g : C ×_S T' ⟶ C ×_S T` satisfies
237	`π_T ∘ g_C = g ∘ π_{T'}` (definition of the fiber product). Therefore on
238	line bundles `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*`, with the equality realised as the

/-- The obvious free sheaf of modules of rank `1`. -/

    (f : unit R ⟶ M) (p : M ⟶ N) :

/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**

A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))

variable [HasWeakSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{v}]

/-- Given a locally bijective morphism `α : R₀ ⟶ R.val` where `R₀` is a presheaf of rings
and `R` a sheaf of rings (i.e. `R` identifies to the sheafification of `R₀`), this is
the associated sheaf of modules functor `PresheafOfModules.{v} R₀ ⥤ SheafOfModules.{v} R`. -/
@[simps! -isSimp map]
noncomputable def sheafification : PresheafOfModules.{v} R₀ ⥤ SheafOfModules.{v} R where
  obj M₀ := sheafify α (CategoryTheory.toSheafify J M₀.presheaf)
  map f := sheafifyMap _ _ _ f
    ((toPresheaf R₀ ⋙ presheafToSheaf J AddCommGrpCat).map f)
      (by apply toSheafify_naturality)
  map_id M₀ := by
    ext1
    apply (toPresheaf _).map_injective
    simp
    rfl
  map_comp _ _ := by
    ext1
    apply (toPresheaf _).map_injective
    simp
    rfl

/-- The sheafification of presheaves of modules commutes with the functor which

instance : (sheafification.{v} α).IsLeftAdjoint :=
  (sheafificationAdjunction α).isLeftAdjoint

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
instance : IsIso (sheafificationAdjunction α).counit := by
  rw [NatTrans.isIso_iff_isIso_app]
  intro F
  rw [← isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso _ (SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R)]
  simp only [Functor.comp_obj, SheafOfModules.forget_obj, restrictScalars_obj, Functor.id_obj,
    toSheaf_map_sheafificationAdjunction_counit_app]
  infer_instance

instance : (SheafOfModules.forget.{v} R ⋙ restrictScalars α).Full :=
  (sheafificationAdjunction.{v} α).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.full

instance : (SheafOfModules.forget.{v} R ⋙ restrictScalars α).Faithful :=
  (sheafificationAdjunction.{v} α).fullyFaithfulROfIsIsoCounit.faithful

end

section

variable [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{v}]

noncomputable instance :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafification.{v} α ⋙ SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R) :=
  comp_preservesFiniteLimits (toPresheaf.{v} R₀) (presheafToSheaf J AddCommGrpCat)

instance : (SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R).ReflectsIsomorphisms :=
  reflectsIsomorphisms_of_comp (SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R) (sheafToPresheaf J _)

noncomputable instance : ReflectsFiniteLimits (SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R) where

    (sheafification.{v} α) (SheafOfModules.toSheaf.{v} R)

def restrictFunctorComp : restrictFunctor (f ≫ g) ≅ restrictFunctor g ⋙ restrictFunctor f :=
  have : (f.opensFunctor ⋙ g.opensFunctor).IsContinuous
      (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (Opens.grothendieckTopology Z) :=
    Functor.isContinuous_comp _ _ _ (Opens.grothendieckTopology _) _
  SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatIso _ (NatIso.ofComponents fun _ ↦ eqToIso (by simp)) ≪≫
    SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr (by ext : 3; simp [← Functor.map_comp, SheafedSpace.sheaf]) ≪≫
    (SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp _ _).symm

    ((restrictFunctorComp f g).hom.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (by simp)).op := rfl

    ((restrictFunctorComp f g).inv.app M).app U = M.presheaf.map (eqToHom (by simp)).op := rfl

If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is

and uses that the tensor product of two free rank-one modules is free of rank

lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}

input.new_string:
/-- **Refining a trivialisation to a smaller open.** If `M` is trivialised on an
open `U` (`M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U`), it is trivialised on every open `W ≤ U`.

The chart-chase is identical in spirit to `LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`:
factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`
and `restrictFunctorComp` to identify `M.restrict W.ι` with
`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
    (hWU : W ≤ U)
    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := X.homOfLE hWU with hj
  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := X.homOfLE_ι hWU
  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
  refine (restrictFunctorCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
  refine (restrictFunctorComp j U.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
  refine (restrictFunctor j).mapIso e ≪≫ ?_
  refine (restrictFunctorIsoPullback j).app _ ≪≫ ?_
  exact asIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit j.toRingCatSheafHom)

/-- **Substrate tensor commutes with restriction along an open immersion.**

For an open immersion `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M N : X.Modules`,
`(tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f)`.

This is the single missing-infrastructure ingredient of `A.1.c.SubT`. It says
the substrate `⊗` (sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor) commutes
with the restriction functor `restrictFunctor f ≅ pullback f` along an open
immersion. Mathematically it factors as: (i) the presheaf-of-modules tensor
commutes with the pullback of presheaves (sectionwise this is the
extension-of-scalars identity
`B ⊗_A (P ⊗_A Q) ≅ (B ⊗_A P) ⊗_B (B ⊗_A Q)` for `A → B`, Stacks 03DM); and
(ii) sheafification commutes with pullback along an open immersion (the
restriction is exact and the small-Zariski sheafification is local). Neither is
in Mathlib at the `SheafOfModules` level (there is no monoidal structure on
`SheafOfModules`, hence no strong-monoidal pullback). A genuine construction
requires either the `Localization.Monoidal` transport of the monoidal structure
through `PresheafOfModules.sheafification.IsLocalization` together with
`W.IsMonoidal` for `W := (J.W).inverseImage (toPresheaf _)` (an instance NOT
present in Mathlib and the real residual obstacle), or a direct sectionwise
construction. Left as a named typed `sorry` feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`;
see the task result for the precise missing instance statement. -/
noncomputable def tensorObj_restrict_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
    [IsOpenImmersion f] (M N : X.Modules) :
    (tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f) :=
  sorry

/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**

If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. The proof picks, for each point `x`,
a common affine open `W ∋ x` contained in trivialising opens `U` (for `M`) and
`U'` (for `N`), refines both trivialisations to `W` via `restrictIsoUnitOfLE`,
then transports through `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, the bifunctoriality
`tensorObjIsoOfIso`, and the unit isomorphism `tensorObj_unit_iso`:
`(M ⊗ N)|_W ≅ M|_W ⊗ N|_W ≅ 𝒪_W ⊗ 𝒪_W ≅ 𝒪_W`. The only residual gap is the
substrate-restriction compatibility `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. -/
lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) := by
  intro x
  obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU_aff, ⟨eM⟩⟩ := hM x
  obtain ⟨U', hxU', hU'_aff, ⟨eN⟩⟩ := hN x
  obtain ⟨W, hW_aff, hxW, hWsub⟩ :=
    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := X) (x := x) (U := U ⊓ U') ⟨hxU, hxU'⟩
  have hWU : W ≤ U := le_trans hWsub inf_le_left
  have hWU' : W ≤ U' := le_trans hWsub inf_le_right
  refine ⟨W, hxW, hW_aff, ⟨?_⟩⟩
  exact tensorObj_restrict_iso W.ι M N ≪≫
    tensorObjIsoOfIso (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU eM) (restrictIsoUnitOfLE hWU' eN) ≪≫
    tensorObj_unit_iso

## tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial (assigned PRIMARY) — RESOLVED modulo `tensorObj_restrict_iso`

For each point `x`: get trivialising affine opens `U` (for `M`) and `U'` (for `N`)
from `IsLocallyTrivial`; take a common affine `W ⊆ U ⊓ U'` via
`exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset`; refine both trivialisations to `W` with
`restrictIsoUnitOfLE`; then compose
`(M ⊗ N)|_W ≅ M|_W ⊗ N|_W ≅ 𝒪_W ⊗ 𝒪_W ≅ 𝒪_W` via
`tensorObj_restrict_iso ≪≫ tensorObjIsoOfIso _ _ ≪≫ tensorObj_unit_iso`.
The whole assembly compiles; `#print axioms` shows `sorryAx` enters **only**

The unit-pullback iso `(pullback j).obj 𝒪_U ≅ 𝒪_W` via
`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit` is the crux of `restrictIsoUnitOfLE`.
Two non-obvious gotchas, both solved:
